The Daily Mail reported that the incident occurred around 2 a.m.

FOX 8 noted that the deceased man was identified as Rodderius Morton. He was allegedly invited into the home by the the mom’s daughter and “came in through the back door at 1 a.m.”

The mom, Kendra Scott, came home around 1:30 a.m. and began asking who was in her house. Morton hid under the daughter’s bed but Scott found him.

The Daily Mail pointed out the daughter claimed Scott then told Morton to “get out of my house b***h.” The daughter said she heard a gunshot when Scott and Morton reached the front porch.

When officers arrived on scene they found Morton dead by the front porch.

WREG observed that Scott told police she found Morton under her daughter’s bed and “did what I had to do.”

The accused now faces a first-degree murder charge.

centerRightText: NEW You can now listen to Fox News articles! Minnesota's flagship paper came under fire over the weekend after a columnist defended Democrat Gov. Tim Walz reasoning for pardoning an illegal immigrant just before the convicted child rapist was set to be deported. "The Minnesota Star Tribune is cheering on Tim Walz protecting an illegal alien monster who raped a 10 year old girl. No one should read this depraved rag. They are sick," said House Majority Whip Tom Emmer, R-Minn., on X. Minnesota's Board of Pardons is composed of Walz, state Attorney General Keith Ellison and state Chief Justice Natalie Hudson. They voted on June 10 to grant clemency to Tou Lue Vang, a Laotian national who between 2002 and 2004 repeatedly raped a child. Lue was set to be deported from the United States before the clemency was issued. WALZ, MINNESOTA BOARD OF PARDONS CLEARS CONVICTED ILLEGAL ALIEN CHILD SEX OFFENDER FACING DEPORTATION In a section of the column called "Cheers to Gov. Tim Walz," writer Rochelle Olson praised the governor and failed vice presidential candidate for his decision to defend the pardon. "The governor refused to back down from his vote to pardon a Hmong man convicted of felony sexual assault in 2006. It’s significant that Tou Lue Vang needed only two of three votes from the state Board of Pardons, but he received unanimous support," the column states. Olson did not mention that Vang's rape victim was a minor, or that Vang raped the child several times over the period of two years. She did reference a letter from the victim addressed to the pardon board supporting Vang's clemency. A spokesperson for the paper told Fox News Digital, "The Minnesota Star Tribune regularly publishes a wide range of opinions across the political spectrum." "In this case, our opinion columnist agreed with a unanimous pardon by the Minnesota state board of pardons, including Governor Tim Walz, Attorney General Keith Ellison and state Supreme Court Chief Justice Natalie Hudson," they added. FORMER INDIANA REP STEPHEN BUYER RECEIVES FULL PARDON FROM TRUMP FOR 2023 INSIDER TRADING CONVICTION Olson launched into a tirade about Jan. 6, 2021, Capitol rioters in her column, likening their actions to that of the foreign child rapist. "Vang’s actions 20 years ago were awful, but so were the violent protests at the U.S. Capitol on Jan. 6, 2021, and those folks received presidential pardons," the author wrote. Author Dennis Neale slammed that comparison in a post on X, writing: "The Star Tribune likens repeatedly raping a 10-year-old girl to protesting in the Capitol on Jan 6. You fluppin’ idiots." TIM WALZ OFFERS STRANGE DEFENSE FOR PARDONING CONVICTED CHILD RAPIST TRUMP ADMINISTRATION DEPORTED "Minnesota’s largest paper actually figured out a way to shove January 6th into this as a way to excuse an illegal immigrant who sexually assaulted a child," said another X personality. "One of the most insane things I’ve ever seen." "The Minnesota Star Tribune just gave a 'Cheers' to Tim Walz for standing by his pardon of a convicted child rapist. Absolutely disgusting," said online commentator Dustin Grage. "The degeneracy of the modern left would be impossible to exaggerate because it is impossible to exaggerate infinity," said Claremont Institute senior fellow Jeremy Carl. Nick Freitas, another online personality, wrote, "Every time I think I despise the main stream media enough, they triple their efforts." Walz's attempts to protect Vang — and his defense of the pardon — proved moot when on July 10, Secretary of State Marco Rubio announced that the illegal alien had been deported to his native Laos. "Just weeks ago, a foreign child rapist was freed to once again endanger America's children after receiving a pardon from Minnesota Governor Tim Walz," Rubio told Fox News Digital at the time. "Tue Lue Vang admitted to committing heinous crimes against a 10-year-old girl in Minnesota. He attempted to pay his victim for her silence and dismissed his acts of child abuse as a 'minor thing.'" "Just days before he was scheduled to be deported, the Minnesota Governor pardoned him, setting him free to endanger American families once again," he continued. "That's why I terminated his legal status in the United States. Vang has now been removed from our country and will never pose a threat to any American ever again." Days after Vang's deportation, Walz doubled down on his decision to grant clemency. CLICK HERE TO DOWNLOAD THE FOX NEWS APP "Did that make us any safer?" Walz questioned. "Did that make the children that are left behind any more stable? Did it improve the idea that we can’t all be judged by our worst day?"
farRightText: There are some controversies so bad that it's just better to sit it out if you disagree with the prevailing sentiment in the country about said controversy. This is one such controversy. Advertisement Let's first catch up: As RedState reported last week in several articles, besieged lame-duck Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz unconscionably pardoned a reprehensible illegal alien for repeatedly sexually assaulting a 10-year-old girl. Walz's vile "logic"? The whacked-out Democrat governor claimed the pardon was based on rehabilitation, the victim’s support, and the idea that the man should not be judged forever by his worst act. Walz also absurdly argued that removing him from Minnesota did not make the state safer. BACKGROUND The Incredible Comment Tim Walz Made About the Deportation of the Child Sex Offender He Pardoned The Logic Walz and MN Pardon Officials Used to Clear Child Sex Predator Will Bend Your Mind Welp, Minnesota's flagship newspaper (left-wing rag) just couldn't leave well enough (horrible enough) alone. If there were still readers willing to give the Minnesota Star Tribune the benefit of the doubt, this might just be the moment that at least some of them decide to stick a fork in the rag. Defending Walz's decision to grant clemency to an illegal alien convicted of repeatedly raping a 10-year-old girl—just before he was set to be deported—isn't merely a controversial editorial position. For the moral among us, it's also incomprehensible. Reasonable people can disagree over taxes, spending, immigration policy, or even the proper use (or abuse) of executive clemency. But when the subject is a convicted child rapist, most Americans don't instinctively begin searching for reasons to justify keeping him in the country. Then again, most Americans aren't like Tim Walz. Advertisement Yet that's exactly the impression the paper left. As reported by Fox News on Monday, the paper was put on blast on social media over the weekend after one of its a columnist defended Walz for pardoning the illegal—just before he was scheduled to be deported. (Spoiler: Rubio deported him anyway.) Minnesota Republican House Majority Whip Tom Emmer summed it up, thusly: The Minnesota Star Tribune is cheering on Tim Walz protecting an illegal alien monster who raped a 10 year old girl. No one should read this depraved rag. They are sick. Sick indeed. Here's the thing: Even if you're a Democrat who supports illegal aliens flowing across our Southern Border, shouldn't there be a point when even you (third-person Leftist) reach a point of disgust, and at least temporarily put your various narratives aside in support of human decency—not to mention defenseless children? Then It Got Worse The Star Tribune went on to dismissively compare the actions of the illegal aliens to January 6 protesters. Under the subhead, Cheers to Gov. Tim Walz, the shameless columnist wrote (emphasis, mine): The governor refused to back down from his vote to pardon a Hmong man convicted of felony sexual assault in 2006. It’s significant that Tou Lue Vang needed only two of three votes from the state Board of Pardons, but he received unanimous support. In addition to Walz, the board includes Attorney General Keith Ellison and state Supreme Court Chief Justice Natalie Hudson. Before her vote, the chief cited the letter of support for Vang submitted by the assault survivor. Also, Vang had not committed additional crimes in the past two decades. Vang’s pardon made him a political punching bag. Secretary of State Marco Rubio deported him to Laos. Vang’s actions 20 years ago were awful, but so were the violent protests at the U.S. Capitol on Jan. 6, 2021, and those folks received presidential pardons. Of the deportation, Walz said, “And by just picking them up and taking them out with no due process, it just seems unfair.” Cheers to Walz for standing behind the pardon. topic: Spain Argentina Extra World Beat 2026

left.title: Spain beat Argentina 1-0 after extra time to win the 2026 World Cup – as it happened

center.title: PHOTOS: Spain wins the World Cup by beating Argentina 1-0 on Ferran Torres' goal in extra time

farLeftText: 18h ago 20.06 EDT Nick Ames It ended almost as Gianni Infantino and Donald Trump had planned. Earlier on a seemingly endless World Cup final day the trophy had, in a scene perfectly of a piece with the event’s excess, been let out of a Louis Vuitton trunk. Now they shuffled up to the podium together and presented it to a beaming Rodri, fumbling the moment slightly when Trump lingered for Spain’s team photo. Within moments they had made their exit, golden glitter sparkling on a bobbly pitch and the reverberations from a football event of unprecedented scale pulsing far beyond this concreted mini-state in New Jersey. How to make sense of a heady, intoxicating, often deeply strange tournament that at once appeared fulsomely welcoming and chillingly distant? It concluded with a disorientating fever dream beneath a bright blue American sky, football appearing a sideshow for much of the afternoon even if the game did not exactly help itself. Anyone straining for a parable could, if they wanted, take pleasure in Spain’s success. This was ultimately the triumph of a collective idea in a World Cup nurtured to indulge the cult of the individual. Trump show offers fitting fever-dream end to a World Cup of excess and damage Read more Share 18h ago 20.04 EDT More from Argentina manager Lionel Scaloni double quotation mark I feel sad. One could say many things about how we got here, but it’s not worth it. Endless thanks to these players who once again took us to the final. If they give everything the way they did today, it sets a great example for ‌the people and for our country. You have to pick yourself up again. When you leave everything like that, it’s very hard to find fault with anything.” [On Lionel Messi] He’s 39 years old now. It was crystal ​clear for me that he was going to play up until he decided not to. I hope that everyone feels proud of him, of what he’s achieved because he’s the best football player ever to set foot on a pitch. I have no doubt about this, and what he did during this World Cup was incredible. But also what he did before this. [On his own future] I will talk to the president. I believe it’s only ‌fair that I can ​take this time for myself ​to think this through. I never thought I would get to this place and it’s wonderful. In order to continue, you need a great deal of things, resetting your mind, rebooting, creating a group like this again – that is very difficult. I am very sorry. Share Updated at 20.05 EDT 18h ago 19.57 EDT Sam Jones Neither the approach of yet another enervating heatwave, nor the prospect of heading to work on Monday morning, was going to keep Madrileños huddled indoors when the World Cup, and the stifling streets of the capital, were there for the taking. With the temperature at 9pm nudging 32C, people across the city and the wider region filled bars, parks, fairgrounds and bullrings to drink, to stripe the streets red-and-yellow with their rojigualda flags, and to test their lungs, their nerves, and, as it turned out, their boredom thresholds. In the capital itself, the faithful descended on a giant screen by the river Manzanares and trooped into the cavernous concrete confines of the 15,000-capacity Movistar arena. Most, however, appeared to have made for the Plaza de Colón to watch the game under the statue of Christopher Columbus, for whom the square is named. Overhead, sharp against a bright blue July sky, flew the largest flag in Spain: a vexillological behemoth that measures 21m by 14m and weighs 35kg. In Madrid fans defy heat, early starts and the torpor of a slow game to cheer Spain to World Cup victory Read more Share 18h ago 19.53 EDT Charles De Ketelaere is the answer to a future quiz question. He was the only man to score against Spain at this World Cup in 12 hours of football. They never went behind, they were only taken to extra-time in the final. In the parlance of our times, they’re a beautiful, brilliant football team. Share 18h ago 19.44 EDT Lionel Scaloni’s reaction double quotation mark They (Spain) were better, that’s the truth. But I’ll hold on to an enormous memory of what they’ve done, of what it’s worth to get this far. We’re great in victory and we have to be great in defeat. Today we’re showing that we know how to lose. We lost the match and we’re owning it. But that doesn’t mean we’ll stop remembering everything we did to get here. Share 18h ago 19.33 EDT Rodri, a giant of a human being and a pretty good footballer too, reflects on Spain’s triumph and his comeback from injury double quotation mark I cannot express it with words. It’s like we’re in a cloud. It’s been a very tough time. I just want the new generations to see my example as an opportunity – when you go down you can rise again. This is my philosophy my entire life. Of course, sometimes things go well, things they go wrong. But always the positivity I said to the lads before the game – we have to go for the game. We need to face them. We have to look to the eyes and see it. We won this game and then life gives you the World Cup or not. But we were brave and I was brave in that moment. Share 18h ago 19.26 EDT Thanks to Francis Fowles for this cracking stat (assuming it’s correct) Only four players have won the World Cup, European Championship, European Cup/Champions League and Ballon d’Or: Franz Beckenbauer Gerd Muller Zinedine Zidane Rodri Share 19h ago 19.19 EDT Mikel Merino’s verdict double quotation mark Unbelievable. It’s the perfect moment for us – we’re such a golden generation with these young players who give everything for this country, for these fans. It was a game about concentration. We knew we were a better team in terms of football. The pitch was dry and they know how to make the game difficult to you. I think the team was outstanding today and, yeah, we deserved it. When you are facing a low block, you have to attack the box with bodies on the line and control the transitions. Luckily we have very good crossers of the ball, big threats in the box. That’s the old-school way of scoring goals and we did it. [What’s the superpower of this team?] The connection. The family we are. Share 19h ago 19.17 EDT Will Unwin ”I thought it was crap,” Wayne Rooney surmised on BBC after enduring the half-time show. Amid tense final, the interval was always going to be prominent, theoretically bringing light to the shade. Why the two UK broadcasters felt the need to transmit the karaoke session in full we will never know but at least the pundits suffered through it, too. Ally McCoist said it had “mentally scarred” him as someone in attendance, unlike many of his ITV colleagues who were sat in Brooklyn. The studio has rightly earned them kudos, creating a home alongside the Hudson River with the Brooklyn Bridge providing the dramatic backdrop. With the game only an expensive train ride away, it was a surprise to see Roy Keane, Gary Neville and Ian Wright in familiar surroundings. ‘It was crap’: Wayne Rooney’s candour helps BBC in World Cup final TV battle Read more Share 19h ago 19.15 EDT Alexander Abnos Donald Trump and Gianni Infantino were booed as they took to the field for the presentation ceremony of the World Cup final on Sunday. Trump arrived at the stadium on Marine One, with the president’s helicopter first flying over the stadium about 45 minutes before kick-off. There was little reaction from the crowd as the aircraft flew over the stadium, with Marine One landing nearby a few minutes later. The president was seated in a glassed-in section of luxury seats on the west side of the stadium shortly before kick-off, along with the Fifa president, Gianni Infantino. Other dignitaries and former players sat nearby, including Spain’s king, Felipe VI, Canada’s prime minister, Mark Carney, and Mexico’s president, Claudia Sheinbaum. Share 19h ago 19.09 EDT Ferran Torres' reaction double quotation mark It was ​a goal scored by 47 million ​people – it wasn’t even mine or from the 26 players. It was destined ​to happen; it was ‌meant to be ​a winner. Whilst ​all finals are tough, when you’re up against [Lionel] Messi, you do get a bit nervous. We played better football. I’ve been heavily criticised throughout the World Cup. Thank God, who always gives me the strength to carry on. God gives things to those who deserve them most. Share 19h ago 19.08 EDT Luis de la Fuente's reaction double quotation mark I’m so ‌proud of this generation of footballers who have grown up with this philosophy, remained true to ‌it, made it even better, and set an example as a team and a family – they’re excellent, world-class footballers with exceptional talent. I’m very moved. Looking back and thinking about them, we’ve won everything, absolutely everything, with this generation of players. The match should have been decided much earlier ... but this is a World Cup final and you have to dig deep even against ten men, and we’re prepared for anything. Share 19h ago 19.07 EDT “I actually thought it was a pretty decent game, as it goes,” says James Humphries. “A clash of styles, in that one team had one, and also some drama and some quality defending. Or has the game gone to such an extent that we think 6-4 is a serious scoreline?” Chill out Jose. Share 19h ago 19.06 EDT “Shout out to 2010 on the bad World Cup finals list?” says Tom Hopkins. “The Dutch did disgrace themselves in slightly more entertaining fashion that day though.” Fair point, but at least the villains (sic) had some genuine chances in that game. Off the top of my weary bald head, I’d say the worst men’s World Cup finals, in order, are probably 1990, 2026, 1994, 2014, 2010. Share 19h ago 19.03 EDT Jacob Steinberg A worrying thought as a tepid first half drew to a close: was the half-time show going to be better than the football? This was a slog. Argentina could not match Spain’s possession approach but they had ways to level the playing field. There was physicality from Lionel Scaloni’s side, whose aim was to kill the spectacle before taking control in the second half, and they were allowed to take it to the limit by the Slovenian referee, Slavko Vincic. Alexis Mac Allister could have been booked for an early foul on Dani Olmo and the leniency, together with a pitch not conducive to playing flowing football, allowed Argentina to disrupt Spain’s passing game, creating the conditions for one of the most tedious World Cup finals. Patience beats overreliance on Messi: how the final was won and lost Read more Share 19h ago 19.00 EDT “Where are we placing this one in the list of all time worst World Cup finals?” writes Kári Tulinius. “My first instinct is to put this second behind 1994, but at least that gave us the tragedy of Roberto Baggio’s penalty miss. I’m already struggling to remember anything about this game.” I’m not sure 1994 was as bad as we remember I can’t see 1990 ever being surpassed. Share 19h ago 18.57 EDT Roy Keane on Rodri double quotation mark He’s got the full package – his decision making, being an option for his team-mates, when to play one- or two-touch. He’s a big strong boy, helps out with set-pieces; he seems like a really top pro. If you’re talking about the greatest midfielders in the history of the game, he has to be up there. He turns up all the time. Every time he gets the ball, he makes the right decision. The guy’s amazing. Share 19h ago 18.51 EDT The World Cup roll of honour 5 Brazil 4 Italy, Germany 3 Argentina 2 Uruguay, France, Spain 1 England Share 19h ago 18.51 EDT Now, Spain players are crowding around a replica shirt with a difference – there are two stars above the crest. Time to hand you back to Rob Smyth for more reaction. Share 19h ago 18.48 EDT Rodri hoists the trophy aloft and the ticker-tape cannons go off. The World Cup is handed around players, then to manager Luis de la Fuente, before coaching staff are invited on to the podium en masse. Every person touching that gold trophy is fulfilling a lifelong dream – even the Spanish royal family look a touch overawed. View image in fullscreen Rodri lifts the trophy! Photograph: Bradley Collyer/PA View image in fullscreen Gianni Infantino carefully explains to Donald Trump that he is not part of the Spanish national team. Photograph: Javier García/Shutterstock Share Updated at 22.00 EDT 19h ago 18.45 EDT King Felipe VI, the king of Spain, is also in the queue of dignitaries lined up to congratulate the world champions. Trump tells Rodri “you’re my favourite player” – and then stops the Spain captain for another chat as he’s about to lift the trophy. Even Infantino looks embarrassed at this point, but Trump insists on standing alongside the Spain players, who eventually focus on the business of celebration. Share 19h ago 18.40 EDT Argentina go up and collect their silver medals, and it’s all too much for Lionel Messi. Having worn a downbeat, bemused expression since the final whistle, the tears now start to flow. It’s almost certain that the greatest player of all time has played his last World Cup match, and that’s sad whoever you support. View image in fullscreen Photograph: Sarah Yenesel/EPA Share Updated at 18.41 EDT 19h ago 18.39 EDT The Golden Boot goes to Kylian Mbappé, although he’s not here to receive any award in person. The France forward scored 10 goals, with Messi unable to overtake him in this year’s chart or the all-time standings today. Golden Boot: World Cup 2026 top goalscorers Read more Share 19h ago 18.37 EDT Rodri wins the Golden Ball And people thought he was over the hill. The Spain midfielder has been back to his metronomic, unflappable best at this World Cup, and a deserving winner. He gets a “well played” from Trump, and a trophy to go alongside his Ballon d’Or. Somewhere in Brazil, Vinícius Jr turns the telly off. View image in fullscreen Photograph: Ulrik Pedersen/NurPhoto/Shutterstock Share Updated at 18.40 EDT 19h ago 18.33 EDT Pau Cubarsí wins young player of the tournament The Barcelona centre-back gets the nod over teammate and clubmate Lamine Yamal, and it’s thoroughly deserved; Spain conceded one goal in the entire tournament. On that theme, Unai Simón has won the Golden Glove – and opts against a repeat of Emi Martínez’s antics in 2022 with the trophy. View image in fullscreen Pau Cubarsi receives the young player of the tournament award. Photograph: Franck Fife/AFP/Getty Images Share Updated at 18.35 EDT 19h ago 18.31 EDT I should also point out that the president of Mexico, Claudia Sheinbaum, and the Canadian prime minister, Mark Carney, are also here for the presentation. We’ll start with the referees, but now they’re handing out some individual prizes … Share 19h ago 18.29 EDT Donald Trump and Gianni Infantino make their way on to the pitch, to plenty of jeers and whistles from the crowd. On the BBC’s coverage, Wayne Rooney labels Infantino’s white trainers “stupid”, which I enjoyed. View image in fullscreen Gianni Infantino with his ‘stupid’ trainers and stable genius Donald Trump. Photograph: Steph Chambers/FIFA/Getty Images Share Updated at 18.51 EDT 19h ago 18.27 EDT We’re getting close to trophy time; there is a huge replica of the World Cup in the centre of the pitch, and now Sergio Ramos is bringing out the real thing. A perfect choice, Ramos: he’s Spanish but also loved a bit of skulduggery in his playing days. View image in fullscreen Sergio Ramos, surprisingly not being sent off while bringing the trophy onto the pitch. Photograph: Carl Recine/Getty Images Share Updated at 18.31 EDT 20h ago 18.18 EDT A replay of Ferran Torres’s goal shows that he was being tracked by Simeone, who then pulled up on the edge of the area with an injury. That gave Torres the space he needed to score. Share 20h ago 18.15 EDT That was pretty intense, so I’m going to take a break and hand over to Niall McVeigh for a bit. Share 20h ago 18.13 EDT Alexis Petridis Like hydration breaks and Donald Trump’s ability to get refereeing decisions overturned by simply phoning up the president of Fifa and complaining, the final half-time show is an innovation of the 2026 World Cup that comes with a degree of controversy attached. For one thing, its existence automatically means the World Cup final breaks the International Football Association’s 15-minute half-time mandate. For another, its obvious antecedent lies in the NFL’s Super Bowl, where the half-time entertainment has mushroomed over the decades from a few marching bands to one of the big pop events of the year. To its detractors, it smacks of America not so much embracing the World Cup as imposing itself upon it. Hits, misses and Muppets: the star-studded World Cup half-time show nobody asked for Read more Share 20h ago 18.11 EDT Modern sport, we’re told, is all about the individual. Your boy has 422 gazillion Instagram followers, blah blah blah. Spain and Paris Saint-Germain, the best teams in the world, beg to differ. The best team usually wins the World Cup, but rarely has the T-word been as operative in 2026. View image in fullscreen Photograph: Ronald Wittek/EPA Share Updated at 18.15 EDT 20h ago 18.08 EDT Matt Hughes Spain finally broke Argentina’s dogged resistance in extra time to win the men’s World Cup for the second time with a 1-0 victory. The Barcelona winger Ferran Torres scored the only goal of a disjointed game that was almost ruined by Argentina’s deliberate spoiling tactics, which backfired when they were reduced to 10 men for extra time by the late dismissal of Enzo Fernández for two yellow cards. Spain win the World Cup after Ferran Torres dethrones 10-man Argentina in extra time Read more Share 20h ago 18.07 EDT Paredes sent off after the final whistle As in 1990, Argentina’s attempt to defend their trophy has ended with two men being sent off in the final. Paredes could have been sent off several times during the game. One of the Argentina players punched a Spanish substitute in the stomach as well. Can you be given a retrospective red card for incidents after the final whistle? View image in fullscreen Genuinely pathetic from Leandro Paredes Photograph: Amanda Perobelli/Reuters Share Updated at 18.14 EDT 20h ago 18.06 EDT Spain have become the first team to hold the World Cup in both men’s and women’s football. Their triumph at this tournament brooks no argument. They’re the purest football team in the world and the best. It took them a long time to break down a fiercely resilient Argentina, but Ferran Torres assumed the Iniesta role by scoring the winning goal in the 106th minute. Ferran Torres Share Updated at 18.20 EDT 20h ago 18.03 EDT There’s some aggro in the middle of the pitch, and apparently Paredes is throwing punches. What a bedside manner. View image in fullscreen Gavi clashes with Argentina’s Leandro Paredes at full-time. Such terrible sportsmanship from Argentina. Photograph: Kai Pfaffenbach/Reuters Share Updated at 18.28 EDT 20h ago 18.02 EDT Full time: Spain 1-0 Argentina Spain are world champions! Share Updated at 18.28 EDT 20h ago 17.59 EDT 120+2 min: Great chance for Simeone! Messi’s near-post corner is only half cleared to Simeone, who is leaning backwards and shoots over from 10 yards! That’s Argentina’s best chance of the game by a mile. Share Updated at 18.38 EDT 20h ago 17.58 EDT 120+1 min Five minutes of added time. Blimey. Share 20h ago 17.58 EDT 120 min: Chance for Argentina! So close to an equaliser! After a short free-kick from Messi, Simeone got round the back and hammered a low cross that was brilliantly turned past the near post by Cubarsi. He had to make that. Share Updated at 18.38 EDT 20h ago 17.55 EDT 117 min Messi collects a bouncing ball 25 yards out and wallops a shot that smashes Merino straight in the coupon, knocking him off his feet. That’s Argentina’s first attempt at goal all night; their xG is now 0.04. Play is stopped while Merino is checked for concussion. Share Updated at 17.55 EDT 20h ago 17.54 EDT 116 min What the hell was that? Williams beats Molina on the left, moves into the area and tries to find Torres with a showboaty flick when a simple low pass would suffice. He makes a total mess of it and knocks the ball out of of a goalkick. If Spain don’t win the World Cup, he’ll regret that for the rest of his natural-born days. Share Updated at 17.58 EDT 20h ago 17.52 EDT 115 min Messi takes a short corner on the right, gets it back and curls a big, dipping cross that briefly startles Simon before landing on the roof of the net. Share Updated at 17.53 EDT 20h ago 17.52 EDT 115 min Ferran Torres is a very good player, not a great one. But he is a few minutes away from joining the best club in world football. These are the men who have scored the winning goal in a World Cup final: Angelo Schiavio, Alcides Ghiggia, Helmut Rahn, Gerd Muller, Jorge Burruchaga, Andreas Brehme, Andres Iniesta, Mario Gotze. Share Updated at 18.38 EDT 20h ago 17.51 EDT 114 min: Torres has a goal disallowed. It looked very tight as he ran through from the halfway line to beat Martinez, but the semi-automated technology confirmed the decision straight away. View image in fullscreen Ferran Torres is a fraction offside. centerText: EAST RUTHERFORD, N.J. (AP) — Ferran Torres' first goal of this year's World Cup was the goal of his life, and it put Spain back atop the soccer world. Denying Lionel Messi a second straight title in what he has indicated would be his final World Cup match, Torres came off the bench and scored in the 106th minute and Spain — in a match it simply dominated — ended Argentina's reign with a 1-0 win on Sunday. Torres pounced on a bouncing ball in the box and used his left foot to bury a shot just under the crossbar and into the back of the net. "It was a goal scored by 47 million people," Torres said, referring to Spain's population. Maybe not by them — but certainly for them. It was Spain's second title, this trophy getting added to the one claimed in 2010. And there's no question about the center of the soccer universe now, not after this display. Spain allowed only one goal in eight matches at the tournament, setting a World Cup record for fewest goals allowed by a champion. And this title, combined with the World Cup won by Spain's women in 2023, makes the European nation the first to hold both the men's and women's titles simultaneously. "I feel sad," said Messi, who was moved to tears after receiving his runner-up medal and staring into the crowd at Argentina supporters. "They were better, to be honest." The score indicates this match was close, but only one team was in command. Spain took the game's first 20 shots on goal before Argentina — desperate for an equalizer — got its first attempt off in the second half of extra time. Spain took nine of the game's first 10 corner kicks, and only a World Cup final-record 12 saves by Argentina goalkeeper Emiliano Martinez gave the 2022 champions a shot at going back-to-back. Spain is now unbeaten in its last 38 matches — 29 wins, nine draws — and now is the outright holder of the longest such streak by any European team ever. Italy went 37 matches (28 wins, nine draws) from October 2018 through September 2021, before Spain ended that streak. And for the last two-plus years, nobody has beaten Spain. Not even Messi. An Argentina team that was the kings of the late-game rally in this tournament had no answer this time. Messi took a corner kick with about four minutes left before the final whistle and the ball took a fortunate skip toward reserve Giuliano Simeone — who sent it high and over the crossbar, holding his head in disbelief afterward. "When you have Messi on the other side, well, you are nervous," Torres said. "At the end of the day, we always depended on us. We always come up with our football, and we showed it again." The win made Spain coach Luis de la Fuente, at 65, the oldest coach ever to lead a team to the World Cup title. "We are world champions," de la Fuente said. "It was together we reached this stage." There was some pushing and shoving after the final whistle, which was quickly cleaned up and Spain commenced its celebration as most Argentina players sat on the turf in disbelief. Cooler heads prevailed, and Spain's players lined up minutes later to form an honor guard of sorts for Argentina's squad to walk through on its way to the stage for the runner-up medals. Enzo Fernandez was sent off in second-half stoppage time after getting his second yellow card, meaning Argentina was forced to finish the match with only 10 players. Fernandez was carded for a reckless play that sent Spain defender Pau Cubarsi cartwheeling into the air, and the card came out immediately. It was the 104th and last match of the biggest World Cup ever, a 48-team event that played out over the U.S., Canada and Mexico. Because there were more matches than any previous World Cup, it was no surprise that there were more goals than all other editions — more than 300, nearly three per match. Except, of course, when Spain was involved. Spain's plan was simple and flawless: Hold the ball as much as possible, denying opponents any hint of chances. It worked, all the way to the end. "There is sadness, but also the knowledge that we gave it our all," Argentina coach Lionel Scaloni said. "I could say a lot about how we got here, but it's not worth it. I am eternally grateful to these guys; they competed right to the end. The other team was better." Spain came into this tournament considered as one of the favorites, and with the full belief it could end Argentina's reign. It had to go through the game's greatest player — maybe ever — to make good on that promise. Messi is 39 and the math certainly suggests that the Inter Miami star is at the end of his international career. There have been hints — such as Messi himself declaring that a home World Cup qualifying match against Venezuela last year would be his final competitive contest for Argentina on his nation's soil. "I experienced a lot of things on this pitch, good and not so good," Messi said that night. "I'll take the good with me." In the Messi era, there was a lot of good. A lot of great, actually. There was the World Cup title in 2022, obviously topping the list. There was the Olympic gold medal at the 2008 Beijing Games. The two Copa América titles, the first in 2021 and the second in 2024. He won Argentina's player of the year award 16 times in a span of 19 years, first winning it as a teenager and continuing to win it all the way into his late 30s. He's a legend within the game and on all corners of the globe. But Spain was better Sunday. "I spoke to my players, and they said: 'Look, we've won everything, coach. We've won everything,'" de la Fuente said. "And that is beautiful." The spectacle was not limited to simply soccer. There was a halftime show, a first for FIFA. There were a ton of celebrities there from stage, screen and sports: Brad Pitt, Tom Cruise, Matt Damon, Cillian Murphy, Daniel Craig, Stephen Curry, Victor Wembanyama, Shai Gilgeous-Alexander, Jalen Brunson, Kevin Durant, Serena Williams, Carlos Alcaraz, David Beckham, Zinedine Zidane and dozens more. They came to see a show. So did 80,000 other people, not to mention perhaps as many as 2 billion watching around the globe. Spain didn't disappoint. No team has won back-to-back men's World Cups since Brazil in 1958 and 1962, and Spain kept Argentina from pulling off that repeat. "We are gracious in victory," Scaloni said, "and we must be gracious in defeat."

topic: Americans Kenya Ebola Travel Sent Disputed

left.title: Seven Americans quarantining at Kenya Ebola facility after US travel ban, says aid group

left-center.title: 7 Americans Sent to Disputed Kenya Ebola Site After New Trump Travel Ban

farLeftText: Seven American aid workers who had been in Congo to fight the Ebola outbreak are quarantining at a new isolation facility in ⁠Kenya after the US government introduced travel ⁠restrictions, the head of a US charity ​employing them told Reuters. The aid workers are the first known people to quarantine at the facility, which has sparked huge opposition in Kenya and is at the heart of a legal case in which a court has ordered the work to be ⁠suspended. Construction continued, however, according to US officials and satellite imagery reviewed by Reuters. Washington’s new policy says American citizens returning from the Democratic Republic of the Congo, where there is an Ebola outbreak, must spend three weeks in a third country before entering the United States. The US government is building ⁠the 50-bed bio-isolation unit on an air force base in central Kenya for asymptomatic Americans exposed to the virus in Democratic Republic of the Congo or Uganda. Many Kenyans are angered at what ​they see as the US offloading the health risk such patients pose. Last month, Kenya’s ‌health minister announced an immediate halt to the facility’s ‌construction after he was found in contempt of court for failing to observe the order to halt work pending a final ruling. “Samaritan’s Purse has seven American Disaster Assistance Response Team staff ‌members there,” Franklin Graham, president and CEO of Samaritan’s Purse, told Reuters in response to questions. “None of them have any symptoms, but they are being quarantined by the Kenyan government for 21 days,” Graham said. A US state department official told Reuters a group of asymptomatic Americans who had served on the frontlines of the Ebola response had “voluntarily moved to the Kenya facility for precautionary monitoring and isolation”. “Kenyan authorities have authorized their movement into the facility under the observation of the US public health service clinicians,” the official said, adding that the decision was taken “strictly out of an abundance of caution”. Kenyan health ministry officials did not immediately respond to calls or requests for comment ‌on the move. A senior Kenyan foreign ministry official said they had no information on it. Another source familiar with the matter who asked for anonymity said that the group had arrived at the site in central Kenya on Monday and were sleeping in army cots in tents. He said some ​had treated Ebola patients at the Christian aid group’s treatment centres in Congo but others had carried out work such as construction, with no direct contact with the sick. “There is one potential high-risk exposure,” he said, adding that their health was being monitored. Kenyan authorities are not allowing the group to leave the facility to travel anywhere else in the country, he added. The often fatal viral disease spreads through direct contact with bodily fluids from infected people or animals. Samaritan’s Purse, which has been promised several million dollars from the Trump administration for the ⁠Ebola response, is an evangelical Christian group that works in disaster zones around the world. It is one of the biggest foreign ​aid groups treating Ebola in Congo and has the largest number of ​Americans there, working closely alongside the World Health Organization to ​contain the outbreak. One of its US staff members who caught Ebola earlier this month was transferred to a hospital in Germany on Monday.

left.title: Trump says Iran ‘will pay’ for killing soldiers ‘many times over’ after Pezeshkian says country fighting ‘full-scale war’ – Middle East crisis live

left-center.title: Iran live updates: Trump says Iran 'will pay' for killing US troops

farLeftText: From 55m ago 13.00 EDT Trump says Iran 'will pay' for killing US soldiers 'many times over' Donald ⁠Trump said ⁠on ​Monday ⁠that Iran ⁠would “pay” ​for ‌killing ‌US soldiers “many times over” after multiple service members ‌were killed ​in ⁠action ​over the ​weekend. The US president wrote on Truth Social: double quotation mark Every time Iran kills an American Soldier they will pay for that killing many times over! This directive has been passed on to Secretary of War, Pete Hegseth, Chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff, Daniel Caine, and every Leader in the Military. The US military on Monday identified two soldiers killed in Jordan while defending against Iranian ballistic missile and drone attacks. An examination of remains is underway to determine whether they belong to a third service member who was reporting missing in that attack. And in Iraq, another US service member died after “a controlled detonation” of an Iranian drone. It brings the total number of deaths in the war with Iran, which the US and Israel started in late February, to 17. Share Updated at 13.01 EDT 33m ago 13.23 EDT Netanyahu will not be arrested while in US, says Trump Donald ⁠Trump also said on Monday that Israeli prime minister Benjamin Netanyahu will not be arrested ⁠when he visits ⁠the ​United States, after New York City mayor Zohran Mamdani said he was still ⁠considering the move. “Benjamin Netanyahu will not be arrested, in any way, shape, or form, while ⁠in the United States of America,” Trump wrote ​on social media , without referring to the mayor’s comments. Trump did not ‌mention Mamdani in his Truth Social post, but credited Israel with helping in the war against Iran. Over the weekend, Mamdani said he was reviewing whether his administration could arrest Netanyahu if as expected the Israeli prime minister visits the city for the UN general assembly in September. “I believe that prime minister Netanyahu belongs in The Hague,” Mamdani told the New York Times’s the Interview podcast on Saturday, referring to the Dutch city that is home to the international criminal court, which issued an arrest warrant for Netanyahu in November 2024 over alleged war crimes in Gaza. “He’s a war criminal who has been charged by the international criminal court, and what you will find is that is an opinion that is held by many, purely because of what his actions have wrought over these past many years,” Mamdani said. Mamdani said it was unclear to him whether he has the legal authority to order the New York police department (NYPD), which he oversees, to detain a foreign leader like Netanyahu. But he said he was in “an active conversation” with the city’s law department about the matter. “Whatever the law allows me to do in New York City, that’s what we will do,” Mamdani added. “But we won’t be writing our own laws to that end.” The warrant which the ICC issued for the arrest of Netanyahu and other Israeli leaders accused them of war crimes and crimes against humanity in Gaza, including “starvation as a method of warfare, and … murder, persecution, and other inhumane acts”. Here’s our story on Mamdani’s comments: ‘He’s a war criminal’: Mamdani exploring whether he can arrest Netanyahu if he visits New York Read more Share 55m ago 13.00 EDT Trump says Iran 'will pay' for killing US soldiers 'many times over' Donald ⁠Trump said ⁠on ​Monday ⁠that Iran ⁠would “pay” ​for ‌killing ‌US soldiers “many times over” after multiple service members ‌were killed ​in ⁠action ​over the ​weekend. The US president wrote on Truth Social: double quotation mark Every time Iran kills an American Soldier they will pay for that killing many times over! This directive has been passed on to Secretary of War, Pete Hegseth, Chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff, Daniel Caine, and every Leader in the Military. The US military on Monday identified two soldiers killed in Jordan while defending against Iranian ballistic missile and drone attacks. An examination of remains is underway to determine whether they belong to a third service member who was reporting missing in that attack. And in Iraq, another US service member died after “a controlled detonation” of an Iranian drone. It brings the total number of deaths in the war with Iran, which the US and Israel started in late February, to 17. Share Updated at 13.01 EDT 1h ago 12.26 EDT Iran’s president says country is in ‘full-scale war’ after expansion of US attacks William Christou Iran’s president has said the country is in a “full-scale war” after the United States expanded its aerial campaign and Tehran struck Washington’s Gulf allies, even as diplomats say talks are ongoing to de-escalate. Iranian media reported strikes around Tabriz, believed to hold underground missile bases, as well as Bushehr, home to the country’s only operational civilian nuclear power plant. The US military said it had struck “military command centers, air defence and coastal surveillance sites, maritime capabilities, missile and drone launch sites and communications networks”. Iran launched attacks against Bahrain, which hosts the US navy’s fifth fleet, and Kuwait, in addition to striking tankers in the strait of Hormuz, which carried a fifth of global oil and gas supplies before the war. Bahrain said its country’s air traffic systems were damaged by the strikes, but said traffic was unaffected. The Iran-allied Houthis in Yemen also announced a blockade of Saudi ports. “The reality is that today the Islamic Republic of Iran is engaged in a full-scale war,” the Iranian president, Masoud Pezeshkian, said in a statement on Monday, warning it might have to endure more strikes. US launches more airstrikes against Iran But in a sign that talks could still stop the escalating round of conflict, an Iranian official told Reuters that mediators gave Iran a proposal for a 10-day-ceasefire to find ways to revive the MoU. The proposal follows a statement by Iran that diplomacy with the US was ongoing despite the fighting, without giving details on the content of the discussions. Iran’s interior minister was expected to travel to Islamabad on Monday, Agence France-Presse reported. “We have been informed by mediators, we have received messages – without going into details – but the main point is that the diplomatic apparatus has been active in recent days and ideas have been conveyed to us by certain mediators,” Iranian foreign ministry spokesperson Esmail Baghaei said at a press conference in Tehran. Hours earlier, the US secretary of state, Marco Rubio, said the US was still open to negotiations with Iran, but that “it has to be real”. “We’re going to continue to respond. If the door opens to diplomacy – if the guys that want to do something productive for Iran win and take control of that system, or take control of the negotiations – that’ll be a very positive development,” Rubio said. Share 2h ago 11.40 EDT France warns of consequences after Iranian security services detain embassy staff French foreign ⁠minister Jean-Noël Barrot said on Monday ⁠there would ⁠be ​consequences after two of its embassy ⁠officials in Tehran were detained for ⁠several hours by Iranian security ​services. Barrot ‌said the ‌French officials were ‌detained without reason for several hours, interrogated and physically intimidated before finally being able to ‌return to the embassy. “I let Iran’s ​foreign minister know that these extremely serious and unacceptable violations ⁠of the integrity of ​our agents ​could not ​go without consequences,” ​he ‌wrote on X. Share 3h ago 11.22 EDT Further to our earlier post on Yemen’s Houthis announcing a maritime embargo of Saudi Arabia, the Iran-backed group did not say how they planned to impose the embargo. Tensions between the Houthis and Saudi Arabia were inflamed earlier this month after Yemeni government planes, supported by Saudi Arabia, bombed the Houthi-controlled Sana’a airport in protest at Iranian efforts to send a plane to the city containing a Houthi delegation returning from the funeral of the Iranian supreme leader Ali Khamenei. The Houthis responded by targeting Saudi Arabia’s Abha international airport. The Houthis’ naval blockade, if enforced, could further strain Saudi Arabia’s economy by cutting off access to its Red Sea ports, which have been vital for oil exports as renewed fighting limits passage through the strait of Hormuz. The blockade is also expected to hurt global markets that rely on Saudi Arabia – the world’s leading crude exporter, producing over 10m barrels per day – for their energy imports. Farea al-Muslimi, a research fellow at the London-based Chatham House think-tank, told AFP “this is an attempt by the Houthis to basically go into a war with Saudi Arabia”. A war “with Saudi shipment is a war with the world”, he added. The Houthis have previously tried to cut off shipping through the Red Sea, leveraging the Bab al-Mandab Strait, a chokepoint at its southern tip which vessels must navigate when travelling to or from the Suez Canal and the Mediterranean. They previously launched drone and missile attacks on ships transiting the strategic waterway beginning in November 2023 during the Gaza war, forcing vessels to take lengthy detours around Africa instead of travelling directly from Europe. “Even if no ships are attacked, the announcement alone is likely to disrupt shipping and create uncertainty for Saudi ports,” Mohammed al-Basha, of the US-based risk advisory Basha Report, told AFP. “Whether the Houthis move from threats to direct action remains the critical issue.” Share 3h ago 10.48 EDT Hamas names Khalil al-Hayya as new overall leader Hamas has named Khalil al-Hayya as its new overall leader, according to a statement from the Palestinian militant ⁠group, succeeding Yahya Sinwar who was killed by the Israeli military in Gaza in October 2024. Hayya, ⁠the group’s exiled Gaza chief and top negotiator, is generally seen as more hardline than ⁠Khaled Meshaal, the head of the militant group’s office in exile and the other frontrunner for the leadership. Share 3h ago 10.41 EDT Lebanese army begins taking over 'pilot zones' in southern Lebanon, state department says The US state department has said the Lebanese army has began taking charge of security in three southern villages under so-called “pilot zone operations”. “Today, pilot zone operations began in the villages of Froun, Srifa, and Zawtar Al-Gharbiya in accordance with the Trilateral Framework and under the auspices of the Military Coordination Group for Lebanon,” the US state department said in a statement. The state department described the step as “a direct outcome” of talks held between Israeli and Lebanese delegations in Rome last week, adding that Washington would keep working with both sides “to implement the framework to a successful conclusion”. Lebanon and Israel announced a “framework agreement” in late June, laying out a plan for Israeli forces to withdraw from southern Lebanon, in exchange for the disarmament of Hezbollah, the Iranian-backed Lebanese militant group. View image in fullscreen Lebanon's president Joseph Aoun shakes hands with the US secretary of state, Marco Rubio, on 18 July in Washington, where he is expected to meet Donald Trump. Photograph: Lebanese Presidency/AFP/Getty Images The Lebanese president, Joseph Aoun, will reportedly present a plan in Washington to Donald Trump tomorrow on how to disarm Hezbollah and secure Israel’s withdrawal from southern Lebanon. Hezbollah has firmly rejected the government’s direct talks with Israel and efforts by the state, which it operates independently of, to strip it ​of its arms. As well as killing many Lebanese civilians in airstrikes across the country, the Israeli military has destroyed dozens of villages in the areas it occupies, something Human Rights Watch said could amount to a war crime. Hundreds of thousands of Lebanese people remain displaced from their homes. Israel has occupied large swathes of south Lebanon in what it calls a “security zone” and says it is trying to protect its northern communities from the threat of Hezbollah. Lebanon was drawn into the war when Hezbollah fired rockets at Israel on 2 March to avenge the US-Israeli killing of Iran’s former supreme leader in Tehran in late February. Share 4h ago 10.25 EDT Kuwait army says it is responding to Iranian attacks Kuwait’s army wrote in a post on X that it is “confronting” missile and drone attacks from Iran, adding that any loud explosions heard will be the sound of air defence systems intercepting aerial attacks. “Everyone is kindly requested to adhere to the safety and security instructions issued by the competent authorities,” it added. Iran’s Revolutionary Guard Corps reportedly said earlier that it hit US military targets in Kuwait, with the Fars news agency cited as saying the Ali Al Salem air base was targeted. The Kuwaiti army also earlier said its air defences were responding to hostile drones from Iran. Share 4h ago 09.43 EDT Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf, speaker of Iran’s parliament, posted on X that the US keeps bringing new military equipment into the region while also claiming they want to end the war. He said Iran has reached a stage of mastery in recognising American games and have prepared themselves as such. Share 5h ago 09.15 EDT Air sirens are again sounding in Bahrain, for the fourth time in less than 24 hours, with the ministry of interior urging residents to remain calm and seek shelter. Earlier, Bahrain’s civil aviation authority said Iranian drones had targeted Bahrain’s air navigation system, but traffic has remained unaffected. Share 5h ago 09.01 EDT Donald Trump says he feels 'very badly' about three US military casualties Shrai Popat While speaking to reporters on Sunday, Donald Trump acknowledged the three US military casualties as the strikes on Iran continue unabated. “Well, we feel very badly, but you know those great people, those great patriots, were out there fighting that Iran cannot have a nuclear weapon,” Trump said while returning to Washington from the Fifa World Cup final in New Jersey. The president insisted that Iran “lost everything almost militarily” and the US controls the strait of Hormuz. “They don’t control anything. So we’ll see what happens. But we hit them very hard again tonight,” Trump added. One service member was killed on Saturday in northern ⁠Iraq during a controlled detonation on a downed Iranian drone and two more were killed in an Iranian attack on Muwaffaq Salti Air Base in Jordan on Friday. The US defence department identified the two killed in Jordan as 25-year-old first lieutenant Tyler James Feehan of Ewa Beach, Hawaii and 19-year-old private Isabella Gonzales of Carrollton, Texas. In total, 17 US military personnel have been killed since the war began. Share Updated at 09.07 EDT 5h ago 09.00 EDT We have some more quotes from the Houthi military spokesperson, Yahya Saree, who has been quoted by Al Jazeera as having said the continued blockade of Yemen’s Sana’a airport “is unacceptable and cannot be tolerated”. Saree said the Houthis would respond “to the blockade with a blockade and to respond to all escalation with escalation”. He continued: double quotation mark The Yemeni armed forces affirm their complete readiness for all options and any foolish act committed by the reckless Saudi enemy. We call upon the people of our great nation to continue the general mobilization and general call to arms, and to be fully prepared for all scenarios and developments, and to support the fronts with fighters. Yemen has been in a state of intermittent civil war since 2015, when the Houthis seized control of Sana’a, the capital, forcing the UN-recognised government to withdraw to Aden in the south with Saudi support, as my colleague notes in this story. A Saudi-led coalition imposed an air and sea blockade on Yemen in 2015, as part of its war efforts to dislodge the Houthis. That year, the UN established a mechanism to inspect and verify that vessels heading to Yemeni ports don’t carry weapons. The blockade has, however, resulted in crippling restrictions on the flow of commercial goods and humanitarian aid into the country, which has been one of the world’s worst humanitarian crises. Share Updated at 09.04 EDT
centerLeftText:
Trump says Iran 'will pay' for killing US troops

Trump says Iran 'will pay' for killing US troops

President Donald Trump issued a warning to Iran on Monday following the deaths of three American servicemembers this weekend-- saying Iran "will pay" every time they kill Americans.

"Every time Iran kills an American Soldier they will pay for that killing many times over!" Trump wrote.

But the president did not elaborate on how Iran will "pay," or whether there will be consequences separate from the ongoing strikes that have hit the country the past nine consecutive nights.

President Donald Trump speaks to reporters after landing in Air Force One, July 19, 2026 in Joint Base Andrews, Maryland. Andrew Harnik/Getty Images

Sunday night, Trump said the U.S. had struck Iran "in honor" of the fallen U.S. servicemembers.

In his post Monday, Trump added that "this directive has been passed on to Secretary of War, Pete Hegseth, Chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff, Daniel Caine, and every Leader in the Military"-- though it is unclear exactly what consequence to Iran is outlined in this alleged "directive."

--ABC News' Emily Chang and Michelle Stoddart

left-center.title: US Fires at Iran After 3 US Service Members Killed in Middle East

farLeftText: From 22h ago 15.31 EDT Centcom says US service member killed in Iraq US Central Command said on Sunday that a US service member in northern Iraq was killed in action on Saturday during a controlled detonation of a downed Iranian drone. It comes after two US soldiers were killed and one missing by an Iranian attack on a military facility in Jordan. Centcom said in an update shared on X: double quotation mark A U.S. service member in northern Iraq was killed in action July 18 during a controlled detonation of unexploded ordnance from a downed Iranian one-way attack drone. A second service member was wounded and continues to receive medical treatment for a minor injury. It added that it had located unidentified remains in Jordan after a service member was listed as missing in action. “An examination process to verify the remains is ongoing,” Centcom said. It brings the total number of US service members who have died since the US and Israel launched war against Iran five months ago to 17. More than 430 US troops have been wounded. Share Updated at 15.35 EDT 21h ago 16.10 EDT The US service member killed during the disposal of an Iranian drone in Iraq on Saturday was not a bomb technician but another soldier who was hit by debris or fragments when the drone was detonated, a US official has told the New York Times. The incident is under investigation, according to the official. It comes after two US soldiers were killed and one missing by an Iranian attack on a military facility in Jordan. It brings the total number of US service members who have died since the US and Israel launched war against Iran five months ago to 17. More than 430 US troops have been wounded. Share Updated at 15.35 EDT 22h ago 15.26 EDT Closing summary The US military launched further strikes after vowing to “swiftly punish” Iran for an attack on Jordan that left two US service members dead and one missing , as fears deepened on Sunday that the war could spiral further into a wider regional conflict. Iran said the United States had attacked an under-construction nuclear power plant in Khuzestan province , in the country’s southwest. The UN’s nuclear watchdog, the IAEA urged restraint and said it was looking into the reports. The US’s allies in the region also reported a fresh wave of Iranian fire. In Kuwait, officials said a power and water distillation plant was attacked for a second consecutive day. Air defences also intercepted strikes in Jordan and Bahrain. Jordan ’s foreign ministry summoned the chargé d’affaires of the Iranian embassy in Amman on Sunday to deliver “a strongly worded protest message” about the attacks. Meanwhile, the US secretary of state, Marco Rubio, met with Lebanese president Joseph Aoun in Washington on Sunday to discuss the implementation of the US-backed trilateral framework. Rubio hailed the country for its “move towards peace” after the latest round of Lebanon-Israel talks. Aoun is due to also meet with Donald Trump on Tuesday in Washington. And the United States was sending more warplanes to the Middle East, the New York Times (paywall) reported, citing US officials who spoke on the condition of anonymity on Sunday, another sign the Trump administration may soon further escalate its strikes against Iran. The redeployment of the aircraft had already been ordered before the Iranian attack on a military facility in Jordan that killed two US service members. Share Updated at 16.25 EDT 23h ago 14.12 EDT Iraqi prime minister Ali al-Zaidi is scheduled to visit Iran later this week following a trip to the US where he met with Donald Trump, AFP reports, citing Iraqi state media, on Sunday. Washington and Tehran are Iraq’s main allies, but their enmity has long turned the country into a proxy battleground and left successive governments struggling to maintain a delicate balance between the foes, who have traded intensifying attacks in recent days. Iraq’s national news agency, INA, quoted the foreign ministry as saying al-Zaidi will visit Tehran “at the end of this week” and is expected to sign memorandums of understanding “to strengthen bilateral ties”. It will be his first trip to Iran since coming to power this year with Washington’s blessing after Trump vetoed another candidate. Al-Zaidi returned to Iraq on Sunday after his one-week trip to the United States, having struck 48 agreements and partnerships with American companies. A businessman, he has vowed to boost Iraq’s fragile economy and disarm pro-Iran groups that have targeted US facilities. In recent days, attacks blamed on Iran have rocked Iraq’s autonomous Kurdistan region, which hosts US troops and foreign oil companies, as well as exiled Iranian Kurdish rebels. Share Updated at 14.16 EDT 24h ago 13.40 EDT Jordan’s Ministry of Foreign Affairs summoned the chargé d’affaires of the Iranian embassy in Amman on Sunday to deliver “a strongly worded protest message” about Iranian attacks. The ministry also took issue with “provocative and inflammatory statements” made by official Iranian entities, it said in a statement. Foreign ministry spokesperson Fuad Al-Majali said the ministry instructed the Iranian diplomat to relay “a clear message” to Tehran to immediately halt its attacks on the kingdom, the statement added. “Jordan’s security and the safety of its citizens represent a red line that cannot be crossed,” he said, according to the statement. Earlier on Sunday, Jordanian air defenses intercepted three Iranian missiles on Sunday afternoon local time while a fourth landed in a remote area, the country’s military said. Share 1d ago 12.12 EDT Further to that last post, Lebanese officials said president Joseph Aoun is due to meet with Donald Trump on Tuesday in Washington. While in Washington, Aoun planned talks “on the situation in Lebanon and ways to strengthen the ceasefire” as well as on “the withdrawal of Israel from the Lebanese regions it occupies,” his office said earlier. Share 1d ago 11.44 EDT Rubio meets with Aoun to discuss trilateral framework As we reported earlier, US secretary of state Marco Rubio met with Lebanese president Joseph Aoun in Washington on Sunday to discuss the implementation of the US-backed trilateral framework, hailing the country for its “move towards peace” after the latest round of Lebanon-Israel talks. Rubio “commended the courage of the Government of Lebanon, under President Aoun’s leadership, for their determined effort to reclaim Lebanon’s sovereignty, disarm Hezbollah and dismantle its terrorist infrastructure, and move towards peace”, the US state department said in a statement. It added that Rubio reaffirmed the US’s commitment to supporting the implementation of the framework. Share Updated at 14.06 EDT 1d ago 11.21 EDT The UN’s nuclear watchdog called for restraint on Sunday after Iran’s Atomic Energy Organization said the United States had attacked an under-construction nuclear power plant in the country’s south-west. The US and Iran have been trading escalating attacks for days, and Washington carried out fresh airstrikes on Sunday to “punish” Iran for the deaths of two US service members in Jordan on Friday – its first reported losses since the return to open hostilities in the war. Tehran’s Atomic Energy Organization said US forces “in an aggressive and brutal act contrary to international law, attacked the under-construction Darkhovin nuclear power plant... with a number of projectiles on Sunday”, according to a statement carried by state TV. As we reported earlier, the UN’s International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA) said it was looking into the reports, noting the plant “is in the very early stages of construction and contained no nuclear material when last visited by the IAEA”. While the incident “is not believed to pose any radiological risk”, IAEA director Rafael Grossi reiterated his “call for military restraint in the vicinity of all nuclear-related sites”, the agency said, in a post on X. It is the first trip to Washington by a Lebanese head of state since Michel Sleiman was received by former US president Barack Obama in 2009. The Lebanese president’s office said Aoun would meet Donald Trump on Tuesday. Aoun, who served as the commander of Lebanon’s US-backed army before ‌being elected president last year, will reportedly present a plan to Trump on how to disarm Hezbollah and secure Israel’s withdrawal from southern Lebanon. Hezbollah has firmly rejected the government’s direct talks with Israel and efforts by the state, which it operates independently of, to strip it ​of its arms. As well is killing many Lebanese civilians in airstrikes across the country, the Israeli military has destroyed dozens of villages in the areas it occupies, something Human Rights Watch said could amount to a war crime. Hundreds of thousands of Lebanese people remain displaced from their homes. Israel has occupied large swathes of south Lebanon in what it calls a “security zone” and says it is trying to protect its northern communities from the threat of Hezbollah. Lebanon was drawn into the war when Hezbollah fired rockets at Israel on 2 March to avenge the US-Israeli killing of Iran’s former supreme leader in Tehran in late February. Share Updated at 10.59 EDT 1d ago 10.14 EDT More than 50 people have been killed in US attacks since the beginning of July, Iran’s Fars news agency has cited the country’s health ministry as saying. IMPORTANT!!!” he wrote on the Truth Social platform in a brief post. The bill would allow Trump to place 100% tariffs on goods coming from the top five buyers of Russian oil ​and natural gas. The legislation is meant to cut revenues from the sale of ​Russia’s energy for its war on Ukraine. Republicans and Democrats have both indicated that the bill could become law over the summer. The White House had previously suggested the scope of the bill could be widened to include Iran and Hezbollah, the Lebanese militant group Tehran has supported and funded for decades. Earlier this month, the US reimposed sanctions on Iranian oil sales in response to attacks on commercial ships near the strait of Hormuz. The waiver was welcomed in Iran, where US sanctions have long crippled the economy. Jordan’s state-run Al-Mamlaka channel also reported that “sirens have been activated in different parts of the kingdom”. In a statement posted to social media, the ministry said the attack impacted a “large number of electricity generation units”, triggering emergency plans to mitigate the effects and “maintain the stability of the electricity grid”. It added that firefighters were working to extinguish the fire while the extent of the damage is being assessed. As my colleague Donna Ferguson notes in this story, Kuwait on Saturday accused Iran of targeting civilian sites and vital infrastructure in the country, such as a power and water desalination plant. Kuwait, which is extremely arid, relies on desalinated water for about 90% of its drinking water, so is extremely vulnerable to attacks from Iran. Share Updated at 08.02 EDT

topic: Criminal Mamdani Arrest Netanyahu Exploring Whether

left.title: ‘He’s a war criminal’: Mamdani exploring whether he can arrest Netanyahu if he visits New York

right.title: Zohran Mamdani Considers Ordering Arrest of Benjamin Netanyahu: 'He's a War Criminal'

farLeftText: New York City’s mayor, Zohran Mamdani, has said he is reviewing whether his administration could arrest Benjamin Netanyahu if as expected the Israeli prime minister visits the city for the UN general assembly in September. Mamdani’s comments on Saturday’s episode of the New York Times’s the Interview podcast echoed ones he had made to the publication as he successfully ran for mayor last fall. “I believe that prime minister Netanyahu belongs in The Hague,” Mamdani said, referring to the Dutch city that is home to the international criminal court, which issued an arrest warrant for Netanyahu in November 2024 over alleged war crimes in Gaza. “He’s a war criminal who has been charged by the international criminal court, and what you will find is that is an opinion that is held by many, purely because of what his actions have wrought over these past many years.” Mamdani said it was unclear to him whether he has the legal authority to order the New York police department (NYPD), which he oversees, to detain a foreign leader like Netanyahu. But he said he was in “an active conversation” with the city’s law department about the matter. “Whatever the law allows me to do in New York City, that’s what we will do,” Mamdani added. “But we won’t be writing our own laws to that end.” The warrant which the ICC issued for the arrest of Netanyahu and other Israeli leaders accused them of war crimes and crimes against humanity in Gaza, including “starvation as a method of warfare, and … murder, persecution, and other inhumane acts”. During his mayoral campaign, Mamdani characterized Israel’s military actions in Gaza as a genocide and Netanyahu as a war criminal, telling the New York Times in September that he would order NYPD to arrest Netanyahu in the event he ever set foot there. He also condemned the 7 October 2023 Hamas attacks on Israel as a “horrific war crime”. The Times report back then cited legal experts who judged that having Netanyahu arrested “would be a practical impossibility” for Mamdani – and could bring him into direct conflict with the US’s federal government. The US is not a party to the ICC and does not recognize its authority. And in February 2025, early in his presidency, Donald Trump ordered economic sanctions against the court, maintaining that it lacked jurisdiction over the US or Israel. Trumps’s US secretary of state, Marco Rubio, recently vowed to “dismantle” the court, accusing it of “waging a war against our country, not with bullets or missiles” but with “the force of so-called international law”. Netanyahu, who faces a tough re-election campaign in October, brushed off Mamdani’s arrest threats in a recent radio appearance, saying he was not concerned. Though Mamdani denies the accusation, Netanyahu also alleged Mamdani supports Hamas. “He’s with the terror actors,” Netanyahu told New York’s WABC. “And I think the problem is that he doesn’t recognize and he doesn’t care that those who hate the Jews and Israel ultimately hate America.” Israel’s ambassador to the UN, Danny Danon, accused Mamdani – New York’s first Muslim mayor – of failing to confront rising antisemitism in his city. Mamdani has countered such accusations by noting that he has increased funding for an office to combat hate crimes. “Israeli Prime Minister Netanyahu will come to New York, address the United Nations General Assembly with pride, and stand before the world to state Israel’s truth and its unwavering right to defend its citizens,” Danon said. Trump’s ambassador to the UN, Mike Waltz, also dismissed Mamdani’s threat as “pure political theater”, saying the US was not a member of the ICC. World leaders from United Nations member-states are expected to travel to New York in September for the body’s annual session. Netanyahu has often participated in this event in previous years. “We’re not talking about a personal assessment of Benjamin Netanyahu,” Mamdani told the Times’ podcast. “We’re talking about the international criminal court and the fact that they have a warrant out for this prime minister’s arrest, and I think it’s important to talk about the weight that that has as a charge and that also, as the mayor of New York City, I’ll be following the laws of New York City.” Mamdani’s comments on Saturday came as his stance on Israel is becoming less fringe within the Democratic party. Close to half of US House Democrats on Wednesday voted to strip $3.3bn in military aid to Israel. It was not enough to pass the measure. But it signified a buckling of unconditional support for Israel, a shift that is also reflected in public opinion polling. Asked by the Times about the political weight he places on Israel and support for it by the US, Mamdani said ongoing war in Gaza was motivating voters throughout the country, including when his endorsed candidates swept House races in New York in June. “It is hard to find a more bankrupt policy approach than what our country has done to Gaza and to Palestine,” he said.

topic: Burnham Speech Prime Minister Pledges Rough

left.title: Burnham pledges to end rough sleeping in England in first speech as prime minister

center.title: WATCH: Andy Burnham says politicians 'need to be better' in first speech as UK's new prime minister

farLeftText:
Andy Burnham has promised to end rough sleeping in England in his first pledge as prime minister, allocating an additional £340m over three years as he seeks to replicate the homelessness schemes he rolled out in Greater Manchester.

Speaking in front of Downing Street immediately after becoming prime minister, Burnham said he would try to get people off the streets “at the earliest opportunity”.

The announcement was welcomed by homelessness campaigners, who say the pledge can be met with enough money and political will.

The prime minister’s critics, however, greeted the promise with scepticism, pointing out that despite having set out a similar goal as Greater Manchester mayor, Burnham had overseen four years of rising street homelessness.

Burnham said on Monday: “On my first day as prime minister, I’m announcing this government’s commitment to finally end long-term rough sleeping across the UK at the earliest opportunity.

“We’ll get to work immediately, bringing together national and local government with the investment needed to get it done.”

Officials said the government would now put an extra £340m into its homelessness strategy – equivalent to £113m a year – which would come from unused parts of the housing department’s budget.

They said the money would pay for 1,200 new homes and help support 3,000 people over five years.

The money comes on top of £3.5bn, the previous government’s allocation for its homelessness strategy, which ministers said at the time was designed to halve the rates of long-term rough sleeping.

Steve Reed, the housing secretary who oversaw that strategy, was sacked from his post on Monday as Burnham began the process of putting his cabinet together.

Burnham’s promise, which the former Labour leader Jeremy Corbyn had planned to announce if elected prime minister, aims to emulate the success of the “Everyone In” policy which the government briefly enacted during the Covid pandemic.

It also echoes a pledge he made when he was running for the Greater Manchester mayoralty in 2017, when he promised to eliminate rough sleeping in Manchester within three years if elected.

8:31 Andy Burnham becomes Britain’s new prime minister - The Latest

After he won that election, he had some early success with his homelessness policy, both with his rough sleeping programme, known as A Bed Every Night, and his broader scheme to tackle long-term homelessness, called Housing First.

In his first five years as mayor, rough sleeping dropped by two-thirds, which supporters say showed the merits of corralling different parts of government around the same aim.

Those in Manchester at the time say Burnham was able to use his position as mayor to force greater collaboration between different councils and government bodies to tackle the problem.

For example, he made the 10 councils under his jurisdiction share data so that someone who was rough sleeping in Bolton, for example, could also be tracked if they came into central Manchester during the day.

He also made sure landlords did not evict tenants because of relatively minor misdemeanours, which experts say is a major cause of street homelessness.

Shabnum Mustapha, a former official at the Greater Manchester mayoralty, said: “In the early days it was hard, trying to bring together a bunch of statutory agencies and organisations which have been working on this problem for decades and decades.

“But by taking a big tent approach, working with different councils, other parties and organisations such as faith groups, he had a lot of success.”

Critics, however, point out that in each of the past four years, rough sleeping has risen, so the level is now three-quarters of what it was in 2017.

Supporters say rates were difficult to keep down after the national government ended its pandemic support schemes. His opponents, however, accuse Burnham of having taken his focus off one of his foundational policies.

James Cleverly, the shadow housing secretary, said: “His record in Manchester showed initial success, then failure once his focus shifted. It looks like he’s taking money from local projects to fund this announcement, so lots of the country will be disappointed.”
